rows.name: Constellation Software Inc.
rows.last_memo: Constellation's stock jumped about 6.5% today with no apparent reason — no earnings, no acquisition news, no management update. For a company like this, where what matters is whether management keeps buying good software businesses at attractive prices and compounding free cash f

rows.name: Deckers Outdoor Corp.
rows.last_memo: Jefferies, a major investment bank, upgraded its rating on Deckers and pointed to HOKA's growth as the main reason. This is good news in the sense that smart external analysts agree with our thinking, but it doesn't actually tell us anything new about the business. The stock is a

rows.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
rows.last_memo: Two minor headlines crossed the tape about Meta's infrastructure plans. First, Meta appears to be building more of its own cloud computing capacity rather than buying it from outside vendors — which makes sense for a company at Meta's scale and is consistent with how they've mana
risks: State and local governments may increasingly restrict data center permitting on energy/grid grounds, which if it becomes a national pattern could slow Meta's AI infrastructure scaling timeline.

Meta's push to insource cloud infrastructure creates execution risk — building hyperscale compute internally is extremely capital-intensive and operationally complex, and vendor relationships may become adversarial as Meta competes with them.

rows.name: Amazon Com Inc.
rows.last_memo: Amazon is building a new highly automated warehouse in Texas and opening up its homemade AI chip — Trainium — to more cloud customers. Neither of these is a surprise; both are pieces of a strategy Amazon has been executing for years. The robotic warehouse means fewer people doing
risks: Trainium adoption requires customers to retool AI workflows for non-Nvidia hardware; slower-than-expected developer uptake could limit the competitive moat benefit.
Heavy capex for robotic warehouses and AI chip infrastructure keeps capital intensity elevated; if utilization ramps slowly, ROIC temporarily compresses before the efficiency gains materialize.

rows.name: Microsoft Corporation
rows.last_memo: A news article noted that Starbucks spends $400M a year on software and is experimenting with AI to build some tools in-house. The concern is that if big companies can use AI to write their own software, they might need Microsoft less. This is a real long-term question worth trac
risks: If AI dramatically lowers the cost of custom software development, some enterprises may reduce spend on standardized SaaS applications at the margin — worth monitoring in renewal rate and net revenue retention disclosures over the next 2-3 years.

The 'build vs. buy' calculus could shift structurally for non-core enterprise applications if AI coding productivity gains prove durable and widespread, creating a slow-moving headwind to seat expansion in products like Dynamics or lower-complexity M365 workloads.
If the weak performance reflects Azure growth deceleration below consensus, that would directly touch the primary thesis driver and warrant immediate re-examination.
If capex is running ahead of schedule without corresponding revenue acceleration, the FCF profile could compress more than modeled in the near term.

rows.name: Alphabet Inc.
rows.last_memo: Google is reportedly running low on AI computing capacity and may need to spend even more on data centers and chips than it already planned. This matters because spending money on infrastructure reduces the cash Google generates in the short term, and the thesis depends on cash g
risks: Capex acceleration beyond current consensus estimates could compress FCF for 2-3 years before revenue from new AI capacity materializes, testing investor patience and the FCF growth requirement of the thesis.
Capacity-driven spending decisions made under urgency (rather than disciplined return-threshold analysis) risk capital misallocation if AI monetization timelines extend.

If competitors face the same capacity constraints, the arms-race dynamic could normalize elevated spending industry-wide, structurally raising the capital intensity of the digital advertising and cloud businesses.

rows.name: International Business Machines Corporation
rows.last_memo: IBM's stock dropped about 3.6% and a couple of prominent analysts issued warnings, with Barron's writing a fairly negative piece about the company's future. The problem is we don't actually know yet what triggered the warnings — whether IBM missed earnings, cut its guidance, or l
risks: Oppenheimer's warning may reflect a specific guidance cut or forward estimate reduction that, if confirmed, would indicate management visibility is lower than previously assumed.

Barron's 'relevance' narrative, if it gains traction in enterprise buyer conversations, could create a self-fulfilling credibility risk in competitive sales situations — though this risk is speculative at this stage.
Net debt/EBITDA at 3.48x already exceeds the 2.5x threshold in our investment philosophy; any FCF guidance cut would worsen this ratio and increase financial risk.

Enterprise customers may be structurally deprioritizing IBM in favor of cloud-native and cybersecurity vendors, suggesting the switching cost moat is less durable than assumed for incremental spend decisions.

rows.name: Palantir Tech Inc.
rows.last_memo: Palantir announced a partnership with a company called SNP that helps large businesses upgrade or replace their SAP software — the kind of back-office system that runs payroll, supply chains, and finance for many Fortune 500 companies. The idea is that when companies go through t
risks: Channel partnerships with integrators like SNP can create margin dilution on co-sold deals if revenue-sharing terms are unfavorable; terms were not disclosed.
Reliance on a partner to drive SAP-adjacent pipeline introduces execution dependency on a third party outside Palantir's direct control.

Nvidia partnership could create indirect dependency: if Nvidia's AI hardware market share erodes or enterprise AI spending decelerates, Palantir's growth narrative tied to Nvidia co-selling could be impaired.

SBA fraud initiatives tied to specific political priorities may be de-funded or redirected with administration changes, adding concentration risk within the government segment if this becomes a material revenue line.

rows.name: Shopify Inc.
rows.last_memo: DoorDash has plugged into Shopify's App Store so that independent retailers can offer same-day local delivery without switching tools. This is good news for Shopify because it means another large, well-known platform is choosing to work with Shopify's merchants rather than buildi
risks: DoorDash's own competitive positioning in local delivery could shift, potentially reducing the value of this integration if DoorDash's network shrinks or pivots away from retail merchant support.

rows.name: Unitedhealth Group Inc.
rows.last_memo: UnitedHealth just reported quarterly earnings and the CEO said the company is 'returning to form' — costs are getting under control and they raised their profit forecast for the year. They also announced they'll buy back more of their own shares. This sounds positive on the surfa
risks: Buyback execution at $432/share (~2x intrinsic value estimates) destroys capital if the turnaround takes longer than management projects, compounding balance sheet risk as net debt/EBITDA approaches 2.5x threshold.
CEO 'returning to form' narrative creates a raised-expectations trap: if Q3 2026 results disappoint, market reaction will be disproportionately negative given the confidence signals sent in Q2.
Without access to the full Q2 transcript, Optum revenue growth rate and segment margin trajectory cannot be independently verified — a key thesis driver remains opaque in this analysis.

Stock price at $432 is approximately 2x base-case intrinsic value; a positive operational quarter does not reduce valuation risk, and any future miss will be punished severely given current expectations embedded in the price.

rows.name: AST SpaceMobile Inc.
rows.last_memo: ASTS just raised $1 billion by selling convertible notes — essentially IOUs that can be exchanged for stock later at a set price. The stock fell 25% when the news broke because investors feared massive dilution, but the actual share count impact is only about 1.5%, which is quite
risks: The $1B convertible note adds a fixed debt obligation with future conversion overhang; if the stock is below the conversion price at maturity, ASTS must repay in cash, which could require another raise.

If the market's initial 25% reaction reflected broader concern about ASTS's commercial timeline rather than just dilution math, the partial recovery may be fragile — any slip in deployment milestones could re-trigger financing-risk fears.
Benzinga's apparent misidentification of the insider buyer warrants verification; if the reported insider purchase is inaccurate, the management credibility signal is weaker than assessed here.
Convertible note dilution risk: if ASTS stock trades below the conversion price at or near maturity, holders convert to equity, diluting existing shareholders at a potentially inopportune moment.

rows.name: Tesla Inc.
rows.last_memo: A financial news site wrote a positive article about Tesla heading into earnings season, focusing on excitement around its self-driving taxi plans. The article contains no new facts — no revenue numbers, no government approvals, no actual taxi rides generating income. Tesla's sto

history.headline: Capex pressure real but thesis-level damage requires evidence of structural margin compression
PLTR-SNP partnership targets SAP migrations; minor thesis support, no valuation impact
UNH Q2 2026: MCR reset narrative + raised guidance, but structural margin compression is real
IBM shares -3.6% on analyst warnings; thin content warrants investigation, not panic
$1B convertible offering extends runway; ~1.5% dilution less severe than market's 25% reaction implied
Robotaxi headline noise; no new data to move a $1.44T thesis built on autonomous execution
Starbucks DIY AI story is anecdote, not thesis-level evidence of enterprise switching-cost erosion
PLTR: Nvidia partnership + SBA expansion reinforce thesis but don't move intrinsic value at current multiples
UNH beats Q2 2026, raises full-year guidance — execution validates thesis but stock still trades well above fair value
ASTS raises $1B convertible notes (2034) + hints at ~$1B Japan subsidy; dilution concern drives -18% drop
CSU up 6.5% in a day; no fundamental event — noise, not signal
Analyst price-target cuts on weak performance; no structural thesis change evident
DOJ expands antitrust probe into Claritev deal, threatening UNH's integration-via-acquisition playbook
Meta AI infrastructure ambitions intact; two capex headwinds are real but not thesis-altering
DoorDash integration extends Shopify's omnichannel reach; thesis-consistent, not valuation-moving
IBM signals customer spending shift; survey confirms weakening demand — thesis under active stress
BlueBird launch imminent + $150M Midland deal + global regulatory approvals: thesis de-risking on multiple fronts
Jefferies upgrades DECK citing HOKA momentum; supports thesis but adds no new fundamental data
Robotics + Trainium expansion reinforces thesis but is incremental, not transformational
IBM pre-announces Q2 'shortfall' — stock -25%; thesis under acute pressure pending detail
IBM's 'Bob' AI platform updates are thesis-directional but lack monetization data to move the needle
ASTS down 7.8% — pure price noise; no thesis-relevant information in this event
NZ regulatory win is real but immaterial; -8% move is noise on a pre-revenue story
